narrative:
**HEADLINE**  
Macro risk rose to 7.5 overall, driven by Japan fiscal deterioration and persistent structural vulnerabilities across credit, supply chains, and geopolitics.

**STORIES WITH LEVEL CHANGES**

• **Japan fiscal / JGB / carry trade** (8→7, High): 30-year JGB yield jumped 50bp to 3.787% and Takaichi plan financing clarity deteriorated to "disorderly" status. Risk of forced deleveraging in yen carry positions and fiscal-monetary coordination failure remains elevated despite modest level decline.

**ELEVATED & HIGH-RISK STORIES** (no change today)  
Private credit opacity at 8 (High), China price spillover at 7 (High), AI power demand at 7 (High), Food/fertilizer fragility at 7 (High), Critical minerals weaponization at 7 (High), Treasury basis leverage at 6 (Elevated), Hidden consumer debt at 6 (Elevated).

**TO WATCH**  
Private credit expansion (level 8) poses the highest tail risk given opacity and interconnection to traditional finance.

stories.name: Treasury basis trade (hidden leverage)

indicators.label: SOFR minus EFFR (%)
MOVE index (Treasury volatility)
SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)

stories.summary: Hedge funds run a highly leveraged arbitrage between Treasury cash bonds and futures. It is a major source of liquidity, but a spike in funding costs or Treasury volatility can force rapid unwinds, as briefly happened in March 2020 and again in 2025.

stories.name: Japan fiscal / JGB / carry trade

indicators.label: USD/JPY (yen strengthening = lower = risk)
30-year JGB yield (%)
Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)

stories.summary: Decades of near-zero rates made Japan the funding leg of a vast global carry trade. As the BoJ normalizes and JGB yields rise, fiscal financing gets harder and carry unwinds can transmit stress worldwide - the August 2024 mini-crash was a preview.

stories.name: Private credit's opaque expansion

indicators.label: US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)
Direct-lending default rate (%, Fitch incl. distressed exchanges)
Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)

stories.summary: Lending has migrated from banks to lightly regulated private-credit funds, where assets are marked infrequently and leverage is opaque. Defaults are rising from a low base; the risk is that losses surface suddenly rather than being visible in real time.

stories.name: Developing-world debt crisis

indicators.label: Broad US dollar index (FRED DTWEXBGS)
US 10y real yield (TIPS, %)
New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A strong dollar, high real rates, and heavy foreign-currency debt loads leave many developing economies vulnerable to a sudden stop. A cluster of sovereign defaults or new IMF programs would signal the slow-burn crisis turning acute.

stories.name: China's price spillover (deflation or cost-push)

indicators.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
USD/CNY (yuan depreciation = higher = risk)
New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)

stories.summary: China's factory-gate prices spill into global goods markets in BOTH directions: years of PPI deflation exported disinflation and squeezed competitors, while a sharp 2026 reversal to positive PPI (energy/commodity cost-push) now exports input-cost pressure. Either large swing is destabilising; the indicator is scored on the size of the move, not its sign.

stories.name: Critical minerals as a weapon

indicators.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)

stories.summary: China dominates the mining and especially the processing of rare earths and critical minerals, and has shown willingness to use export licensing as leverage. Supply chains for magnets and defense inputs have little near-term substitute.

stories.name: Stablecoins & Treasury demand

indicators.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Dollar stablecoins have become sizeable holders of short-term Treasuries, linking crypto plumbing to the funding market. A large redemption wave could force rapid T-bill selling; the GENIUS Act's implementation is the key regulatory variable.

stories.name: AI build-out circular financing

indicators.label: US investment-grade OAS (%) - big-issuer proxy
AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)

stories.summary: AI infrastructure spending vastly exceeds current AI revenue, increasingly financed with debt and circular vendor arrangements. If monetization disappoints or GPU-backed financing strains, the capex cycle could reverse sharply.

stories.name: CRE maturity wall / regional banks

indicators.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A wall of commercial real-estate loans - especially office - matures into higher rates and lower valuations, concentrated at regional banks. Rising delinquencies and 'extend and pretend' practices defer, rather than resolve, the losses.

stories.name: France / eurozone fragility

indicators.label: OAT-Bund 10y spread (%)
Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)

stories.summary: France combines a wide deficit, a fragmented parliament, and recurring budget standoffs, widening its spread over Germany. Political instability or a rating downgrade would test eurozone cohesion at its second-largest economy.

stories.name: Hidden consumer debt

indicators.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
US unemployment rate (%)
BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)

stories.summary: Beneath headline figures, subprime auto and buy-now-pay-later delinquencies are climbing, and the labor market is the swing factor. A rise in unemployment would convert stretched-but-current borrowers into defaults quickly.

stories.name: Gold / de-dollarization

indicators.label: Gold price (USD/oz)
USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)

stories.summary: Central banks are accumulating gold and building non-dollar settlement arrangements at the margin. It is a slow erosion rather than a sudden shift, but reserve 'weaponization' events can accelerate diversification away from the dollar.

stories.name: Insurance retreat

indicators.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
US single-family mortgage delinquency (%, FRED DRSFRMACBS)
Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)

stories.summary: Insurers are retreating from climate-exposed markets through non-renewals and exits, shifting risk onto homeowners and state backstops. Severe catastrophe seasons strain the system and feed into housing and mortgage stress.

stories.name: Pensions / demographics

indicators.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
Large public-pension funded ratio (%)

stories.summary: Aging demographics and underfunded public pensions create slow fiscal pressure, while leveraged liability-driven strategies can blow up fast - as the UK gilt/LDI crisis showed in 2022. Benefit-reform fights are the political pressure valve.

stories.name: AI power & the grid bottleneck

indicators.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)

stories.summary: Data-center electricity demand is colliding with multi-year grid interconnection queues and slow capacity additions. Whether this is an acute shortfall or eases depends on the pace of AI build-out versus new supply - forecasts diverge widely.

stories.name: Copper & the electrification deficit

indicators.label: Copper price (USD/lb, COMEX HG=F)
Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)

stories.summary: Electrification and AI infrastructure point to structural copper deficits as demand outruns new mine supply, which takes a decade to build. Price is the live signal; mine disruptions and inventory draws would confirm tightening.

stories.name: Water - the underpriced input

indicators.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)

stories.summary: Water is systematically underpriced yet essential to chip fabrication, agriculture, and power. Droughts in key hubs (Taiwan's fabs, major farming regions) and transboundary disputes can disrupt supply chains in ways markets rarely price in advance.

stories.name: Food & fertilizer fragility

indicators.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)

stories.summary: Food security depends on concentrated fertilizer supply chains - potash, phosphate, and gas-derived nitrogen - exposed to export curbs and shipping chokepoints. Curbs or input shocks feed through to prices with a lag.

stories.name: The lithography (ASML) chokepoint

indicators.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)

stories.summary: ASML is the sole maker of EUV lithography machines, with Zeiss the sole optics supplier - a single point of failure for advanced chips. Export controls, retaliation, or any disruption to this chain would ripple across the entire tech economy.

stories.name: Sea lanes & undersea cables

indicators.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)
Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)

stories.summary: A few maritime chokepoints and undersea cables carry most global trade and data. The 2026 Hormuz/Red Sea disruptions showed how quickly closures spike energy and shipping costs; cable cuts are a parallel, under-watched vulnerability.

stories.name: Clearinghouses (financial chokepoint)

indicators.label: Equity volatility (VIX)
Treasury volatility (MOVE)
Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Central counterparties now concentrate the system's risk by design, and Treasury central-clearing mandates will route more activity through them. In a volatility spike, procyclical margin calls can amplify stress - they are a systemic single point of failure.

items.label: Direct-lending default rate (%, Fitch incl. distressed exchanges)
items.rationale: Fitch's inclusive PCDR (counting distressed exchanges, PIK shifts, and stressed maturity extensions) held at 6.0% for both April and May 2026 — the rubric anchor — confirming the record level has not retreated; no subsequent Fitch PCDR print was accessible, so the May 2026 reading is retained per the no-anchor-switch rule.
items.fact: Fitch Ratings reported its U.S. Private Credit Default Rate (PCDR) remained at a record 6.0% in May 2026, unchanged from April 2026 (the prior record high), logging 14 default events with maturity extensions under stress dominant. Source: economiccollapse.report citing Fitch Ratings / Epoch Times, published ~June 20 2026; corroborated by Forbes (May 24 2026) and eciks.org (June 17 2026) citing Fitch's May 2026 report.

dissent.view: Proskauer Private Credit Default Index: 2.73% in Q1 2026 (count-based, 697 loans, $189.2B) — excludes most distressed exchanges and PIK events, structurally 2-3 pts below Fitch
Moody's: excluding distressed restructurings yields headline rates of 1.6%-4.7%; including them shows materially worse picture — no single point estimate published
Fitch PMR (privately monitored ratings only, full-year 2025): 9.2% — different sub-index, not the PCDR composite

items.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: At least 5 named funds with combined AUM well above $100B are enforcing quarterly repurchase caps (redemption gates) simultaneously in Q2 2026, satisfying the WIDESPREAD threshold of >=3 largest funds or combined AUM >$100B. The gating is corroborated by SEC filings (BCRED SC TO-I/A), investor letters cited by PitchBook and CNBC, and CRS congressional reporting, with no material source disagreement on the breadth of the phenomenon.
items.fact: In Q2 2026, at least 5+ of the largest non-traded private credit BDCs are actively enforcing 5% quarterly repurchase caps: Blackstone BCRED (~$79B AUM, 10% requests capped at 5%, SEC filing/investor letter June 4), Cliffwater CCLFX (~$31B, 17% requests capped at 5%, PitchBook June 4), Apollo Debt Solutions (~$25B, 16.8% requests capped at 5%, CNBC June 23 SEC filing), BlackRock/HPS HLEND (~$26B, ~13% requests capped at 5% for second straight quarter, ZeroHedge/Bloomberg June 12), and Morgan Stanley North Haven (11.6% requests capped at 5%, PitchBook June 23). PitchBook (June 4) confirmed 'non-traded BDCs managed by Apollo, HPS, Cliffwater, Monroe and Blackstone have all received elevated redemption requests and all held quarterly repurchases at a 5% limit.' Combined gated AUM exceeds $200B. Congress.gov CRS (IN12674) confirmed 'multiple redemption restrictions by private credit funds in early 2026 have generated widespread concerns and congressional attention.'

items.label: SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)
items.rationale: One official extension has been granted (Feb 2025), placing the indicator squarely at rubric level 1 ('an official extension granted'). Additionally, exemptive-relief requests from SIFMA and IIB are pending as of the comment deadline (May 29, 2026), satisfying a second independent rubric-1 criterion; no second formal deadline extension has been announced, precluding level 2.
items.fact: The SEC granted a first one-year extension on Feb 25, 2025 (Exchange Act Release No. 34-102487), moving cash deadline to Dec 31, 2026 and repo to Jun 30, 2027. As of Apr 17, 2026, the SEC published for comment exemptive-relief requests from SIFMA (inter-affiliate exemption expansion) and IIB (extraterritorial scope), with comments due May 29, 2026 — both requests remain pending with no resolution publicly confirmed as of Jul 13, 2026. Commissioner Uyeda stated Sep 30, 2025 that 'the Commission does not intend to consider further extensions.' The SEC implementation page (updated ~Jul 6, 2026) shows deadlines unchanged at Dec 31, 2026 / Jun 30, 2027. No second extension has been announced. Sources: sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-43; sec.gov/newsroom/speeches-statements/uyeda-093025; sec.gov/newsroom/speeches-statements/uyeda-update-sec-work-toward-treasury-clearing-implementation-042026; home.treasury.gov/system/files/221/TBACCharge1Q22026.pdf (May 2026).

dissent.view: Commissioner Uyeda stated Sep 30, 2025 that 'the Commission does not intend to consider further extensions,' and the TBAC May 2026 report notes industry 'is resolved to meeting the central clearing deadlines' — suggesting implementation may remain on the current (extended) track without further slippage toward level 2.

items.label: Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
items.rationale: The rubric's level 2 threshold of a >50bps yield spike on fiscal concern is met: the 10y JGB surged ~80 bps from March to July 2026, peaking at a 30-year high of ~2.90%, explicitly tied to Takaichi fiscal expansion fears and requiring emergency jawboning. Auctions have not failed (30y BTC 4.55x on July 7), but the >50bps fiscal-driven yield spike alone satisfies the OR condition for level 2 under the rubric.
items.fact: The 10-year JGB yield rose from ~2.1% in March 2026 to 2.900% intra-week by July 9-10, 2026 (~+80 bps), explicitly driven by fiscal expansion concerns under PM Takaichi; Finance Minister Katayama had to jawbone the market lower via GPIF remarks. The July 7 30-year JGB auction cleared at a 4.55x bid-to-cover. Sources: MUFG Research Japan Economic & Financial Weekly (July 13, 2026); TradingEconomics citing Reuters (July 9-10, 2026); Dukascopy (July 7, 2026).

dissent.view: Auctions are still clearing well (30y BTC 4.55x on July 7, 2026) and the June supplementary budget was smaller than feared, causing a slight yield retreat; Oxford Economics and State Street view the situation as elevated but not disorderly.

items.label: Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)
items.rationale: Rubric score 1 (FRICTION) maps squarely: TBAC explicitly flags a documentation/legal-agreement backlog and two pending exemptive-relief requests that have not been granted, which is the textbook 'documentation backlog / pending exemptive relief' condition; no deadline extension has been announced and no operational failure in testing has been reported, so score 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: TBAC Q2-2026 charge paper (May 2026, home.treasury.gov): 'Largest challenge from an implementation standpoint continues to be the amount of legal work and documentation associated with the bilateral agreements between participants'; 'there are still open issues that need to be addressed well in advance of the deadline', specifically two unresolved scoping issues - inter-affiliate exemption and extraterritorial reach of the rule on non-U.S. entities - with exemptive-relief comment periods still open as of May 29 2026. The SEC itself has not granted final relief on either issue. Deadlines remain intact at cash 2026-12-31 and repo 2027-06-30 (extended once already in Feb 2025). On June 18 2026 the SEC granted conditional exemptive relief only for private-fund captive-clearing-subsidiary transactions; the two principal scoping exemptions remain pending.

dissent.view: Cash market characterised as 'relatively better positioned vs repo' with significant FICC volume growth (FICC sponsored repo/GC up >150% in two years); BofA and State Street describe existing clearing models as operational - suggesting nearer-to-0 friction for cash leg specifically

items.label: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
items.rationale: Capex growth (~80% YoY) materially exceeds AI-revenue growth (~28-63% YoY for cloud/AI segments), satisfying the 'capex growth exceeds AI-revenue growth' condition; however, AI revenue is still strongly accelerating YoY (AWS fastest in 15 quarters, Google Cloud +63%, MSFT AI +123%), with no hyperscaler cutting its AI-revenue outlook, so the rubric's Score 2 trigger (QoQ deceleration or outlook cut) is not met — this maps cleanly to Score 1 (WIDE).
items.fact: Q1 2026: combined hyperscaler quarterly capex ~$129.8B, up ~80% YoY (MSFT +84% to $30.88B, GOOGL +111% to $35.67B, AMZN +77% to $44.2B, META +54% to $20B); AI-attributable revenue growing but at lower rates — AWS +28% YoY ($37.6B, fastest in 15 quarters), Google Cloud +63% YoY, MSFT AI run rate >$37B annualized (+123% YoY); Allianz Research notes a ~46% divergence between capex and revenue growth; 3 of 4 hyperscalers raised 2026 capex guidance at Q1 earnings. Sources: siliconanalysts.com (2026-07-10), heygotrade.com (2026-04-30), forbes.com/jasonkirsch (2026-06-02), allianz-trade.com (2026-03-25).

dissent.view: Some analysts (Sequoia Cahn, Allianz Research) frame the ~$600B revenue gap and 46% capex-vs-revenue divergence as more alarming than a simple 'wide' characterization, suggesting structural monetization risk closer to a Score 2 concern; however, no single hyperscaler cut its AI-revenue outlook or showed QoQ deceleration in Q1 2026.

items.label: BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: Affirm 30+ DPD rose +29 bps YoY to 2.8% — above zero but well below the >100 bps spiking threshold — satisfying the RISING condition; provider charge-offs (~3.5% cohort NCO for Affirm, <1% for Klarna) remain below the >5% SPIKING threshold, confirming level 1.
items.fact: Affirm FQ3'26 Shareholder Letter (SEC 8-K, May 7 2026): '30+ day delinquencies excluding Peloton and Pay in X loans increased 29 basis points year over year and increased 7 basis points quarter over quarter to 2.8%.' Allowance for credit losses = 6.0% of loans held for investment; recent cohort NCOs tracking ~3.5% of cohort GMV. Klarna Q1 2026 (SEC 6-K): delinquency rates 'continue to trend down,' realized losses ~0.44% of GMV.

dissent.view: Statista notes Affirm 30+ DPD 'increased in Q2 2026' (Dec 31 2025 quarter) by an undisclosed amount YoY, suggesting a possible uptick beyond the FQ3 figure, but the exact magnitude is paywalled and cannot override the audited 8-K data.

items.label: Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)
items.rationale: YTD 2026 insured catastrophe losses are running well below the 10-year average with no single event breaching the $10bn threshold, satisfying both conditions for NORMAL (score 0) under the rubric. The 2026 Atlantic hurricane season is below-forecast and has produced no material US landfall losses through the assessment date.
items.fact: Global insured losses from natural disasters totaled $20 billion in Q1 2026 — 26% below the 10-year average of ~$27 billion — with no single event exceeding $10 billion; Q2 2026 SCS activity added only mid-single-digit billions cumulatively and no major hurricane landfall has occurred through July 13, 2026 (Gallagher Re Q1 2026 Natural Catastrophe and Climate Report, April 2026; Goldman Sachs/Gallagher Re Q2 estimates, July 2026).

dissent.view: Aon calculates Q1 2026 global insured losses of $20bn as 6% above the 21st-century average (rather than 26% below the decadal average used by Gallagher Re), reflecting different baseline periods — still well within NORMAL bounds

items.label: Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Two failures in the trailing 6 months, each <$10B assets, with credible reporting of real-estate exposure contributing to the first failure and a public FDIC consent order preceding the second — this satisfies the ISOLATED threshold of '1-2 such failures each <$10bn assets OR >=1 new public enforcement action/consent order.' No failure exceeds $10B and no coordinated interagency emergency action exists, so CLUSTER (2) is not met. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: FDIC failed-bank list (updated July 10, 2026) shows exactly 2 failures in 2026: Metropolitan Capital Bank & Trust (Chicago, IL; $261M assets; Jan. 30, 2026) and Community Bank and Trust - West Georgia (LaGrange, GA; $288M assets; May 1, 2026). Both are <$10B. Bisnow (Feb. 2, 2026) documents CRE/real-estate loan exposure as a contributing factor in Metropolitan's failure; KBRA (May 2026) confirms a Jan. 12, 2026 FDIC consent order was issued against the Georgia bank before its failure. FDIC.gov; KBRA.com; Bisnow.

dissent.view: KBRA characterizes Metropolitan Capital's primary cause as C&I charge-offs ($13.9M), not CRE concentration per se, which could support a lower reading if CRE linkage is deemed insufficiently direct for the first failure.

items.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
items.rationale: The Fitch ABS index is the primary hard-data benchmark for subprime auto 60+ day delinquency; February 2026's 6.80% is the latest reported monthly figure, mechanically mapping to a value of 6.8 per the rubric.
items.fact: Fitch Ratings subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency index: 6.90% in January 2026 (32-year record), easing to 6.80% in February 2026 — the most recent monthly figure published by Fitch as of the May 2026 Wolf Street analysis (wolfstreet.com, 2026-05-19).

dissent.view: Some secondary sources round January 2026 figure to 6.8% rather than 6.90%, and December 2025 was reported at 6.74% (Auto Finance News, 2026-01-16).

items.label: Large public-pension funded ratio (%)
items.rationale: The Milliman PPFI is the most timely authoritative tracker of the 100 largest U.S. public plans and maps directly to the rubric's request for a representative large-plan funded ratio; 87.6% is the most recent published monthly figure. Equable Institute's broader universe (all state/local plans) yields 82.5% as of Dec 31, 2025, reflecting a different scope and older date, and is recorded as dissent.
items.fact: Milliman Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI): funded ratio of the 100 largest U.S. public defined benefit plans rebounded to 87.6% as of April 30, 2026, up from 83.7% at end of March 2026; plan assets stood at $6.009 trillion vs. total pension liability of $6.860 trillion. Source: Milliman PPFI release, June 3, 2026 (milliman.com/en/insight/public-pension-funding-index-april-2026 and businesswire.com/news/home/20260603397893/en/).

dissent.view: Equable Institute reports aggregate funded ratio for all U.S. state and local pension plans at 82.5% as of December 31, 2025, with a $1.27 trillion funding shortfall — a broader universe and earlier date than the Milliman PPFI reading.

items.label: New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: The window contains 1-2 new IMF arrangements (Mauritania new ECF/EFF; Ghana new PCI) and zero missed coupons or standstills, which maps squarely to the RISING band (1-2 new arrangements OR a single missed coupon). The evidence does not support CLUSTER (>=3 new distress programs).
items.fact: In the trailing 6 months (Jan–Jul 2026), IMF.org press releases show: (1) Mauritania received a genuinely new 42-month ECF/EFF arrangement approved June 24, 2026 (imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/06/24/pr26223); (2) Ghana reached staff-level agreement on a new 36-month non-financing Policy Coordination Instrument (May 15, 2026, imf.org africa-newsroom); all other actions were reviews/extensions of pre-existing arrangements (Ethiopia, Egypt, Nepal, Ecuador, The Gambia). No sovereign missed coupon or standstill was identified in any source for this period.

dissent.view: Ghana's new instrument is a non-financing PCI, not a lending program, and some analysts may not count it as a distress-driven arrangement; under that reading only Mauritania qualifies, still keeping the indicator at RISING rather than CALM.

items.label: Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)
items.rationale: The Russia CBR freeze is a 2022 legacy action with no new confiscation executed in 2026, ruling out rubric-2 on that front; no other new sovereign seizure event was found. BRICS Pay/Unit are live in pilot/early-operational form — beyond rhetoric but not yet a major-economy reserve settlement shift — placing the indicator firmly at rubric-1.
items.fact: As of mid-2026, no new sovereign reserve freeze or seizure beyond the pre-2022 Russian CBR immobilisation has been executed; the ~$280bn in Russian assets remain frozen (not confiscated) per REPO Task Force estimates (Brookings, Jun 2025; CFR, Nov 2025). On non-dollar settlement, BRICS Pay entered an 'operational phase' in 2026 linking central banks via Brazil's Pix/DCMS architecture and the BRICS Unit (40% gold-backed token) ran a pilot in Oct 2025, with full deployment targeted at the India BRICS summit (bricspayment.ru; watcher.guru, Feb 2026; EBC Financial, Apr 2026). No major economy has operationally moved sovereign reserve settlement off USD at scale; EBC Financial (Apr 2026) confirms 'official BRICS documents still point to payments cooperation and interoperability, not a live shared currency.'

dissent.view: Some pro-BRICS outlets (informedclearly.com, watcher.guru) characterise BRICS Pay as already 'operational' and claim intra-bloc local-currency trade has surpassed 67%, which could argue for a rubric-2 reading on the settlement-shift dimension.

GIS Reports and bricspayment.ru (citing Freedom Finance Global) project a working prototype only in 2027-2028 with full operational phase near 2030, arguing current activity remains in pilot stages — potentially a rubric-0 reading for the settlement dimension.

items.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
items.rationale: The 2026 Trustees Report worsened the depletion date by one quarter (to Q4 2032) and materially widened the 75-year deficit, satisfying rubric band 1 ('trustees report worsens the depletion date or reform actively debated'). No automatic cut has been triggered and no legislation enacting cuts has been enacted, so band 2 is not met.
items.fact: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026) projects the OASI Trust Fund will be depleted in Q4 2032 — one quarter earlier than last year's report — at which point only 78% of scheduled benefits would be payable; the 75-year actuarial deficit worsened to 4.42% of taxable payroll (up from 3.82%). No benefit cut has been triggered or enacted. The Bipartisan Social Security Commission Act (H.R. 9187) was introduced in Congress but not enacted. Source: SSA Office of the Actuary, https://www.ssa.gov/oact/trsum/, June 9, 2026.

dissent.view: AARP and combined-fund analysts emphasize the OASDI combined depletion date held steady at 2034 (unchanged from 2025 report), suggesting headline deterioration is modest on a combined basis.

items.label: USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
items.rationale: The rubric requires the latest IMF COFER USD share of allocated global FX reserves as a percent; the July 1, 2026 IMF Data Brief is the most recent official release and directly reports 57.13% for 2026Q1. This figure maps mechanically and without ambiguity to the requested indicator.
items.fact: IMF COFER Data Brief (July 1, 2026): 'The share of US dollar holdings in foreign exchange reserves increased to 57.13 percent in 2026Q1, from 56.42 percent in 2025Q4.' Source: https://data.imf.org/en/news/imf%20data%20brief%20july%201

dissent.view: coferdata.com aggregator shows USD at 56.77% — this reflects the prior quarter (2025Q4) per the March 27, 2026 IMF Data Brief and has since been superseded by the 2026Q1 release.

items.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
items.rationale: The signed YoY figure is +4.1%, the highest since July 2022, driven by energy and materials cost-push from Middle East supply disruptions; rubric treats this as a large positive move signaling input-cost pressure export to global supply chains. All major secondary sources (Xinhua, CNBC, Reuters pool) confirm +4.1% with zero dissent.
items.fact: NBS official press release (stats.gov.cn/english/PressRelease/202607/t20260710_1964093.html, released 2026-07-10): 'In June 2026, China's producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 4.1% year on year and decreased by 0.3% month on month.'

items.label: Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
items.rationale: Within the trailing 3 months, the US side enacted new tightening measures (MATCH Act proposal targeting DUV/ASML, EUV compliance investigation) AND China enacted formal countermeasures (MOFCOM export control blacklist of 10 US firms, MOF procurement ban on 46 US firms, extraterritorial-jurisdiction counter-regulations), satisfying rubric level 2 RETALIATION. The Chinese measures are legally enacted and primary-source confirmed via MOFCOM; market signal corroboration: ASML China revenue share fell from 36% to 19% Q4→Q1.
items.fact: On June 22, 2026, China's MOFCOM added 10 US firms (incl. MP Materials, USA Rare Earth, 8 defense contractors) to its export control list and MOF barred procurement from 46 US defense firms — explicitly retaliating for the Pentagon's June 8 1260H list expansion. In parallel, the US MATCH Act (introduced April 3, 2026) would extend DUV bans to cover ASML sales to China, and US Commerce Secretary Lutnick opened formal inquiries with ASML in April 2026 over suspected EUV diversion. China also promulgated Regulations on Countering Improper Extraterritorial Jurisdiction in April 2026, enabling Beijing to deem ASML's compliance with US FDP rules illegal. Sources: MOFCOM announcement via Arnold & Porter (2026-06-22); Al Jazeera (2026-06-22); CNBC (2026-04-07); JustSecurity (2026-07-08); Yahoo Finance/ASML (2026-06-~20).

dissent.view: East Asia Forum (March 2026) argued the White House was 'cooling' chip export controls and unlikely to introduce new rules amid trade negotiations, suggesting escalation read may be overstated at executive level even as Congress pushes harder

Arnold & Porter (June 2026) noted China's June 22 measures 'may signal China's view that avoiding significant escalation at present is more helpful,' suggesting Beijing sees its moves as calibrated/limited rather than full retaliation

items.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
items.rationale: Traffic has collapsed to ~39% of normal (and likely lower in the Jul 10-13 window) under active US-Iran military exchanges and Iran's declared closure, placing it firmly in 'contested/partially disrupted' territory; however, the Omani corridor remains nominally operational per JMIC, technical talks continued Jul 11, and Brent at ~$79/bbl - elevated but far below the war-peak - does not corroborate a full commercial closure, capping the rating at 1 per rubric.
items.fact: IMF PortWatch (Jul 5, primary source) recorded 34 Hormuz transits vs ~88/day normal (~39% of pre-crisis volume); Windward recorded just 6 crossings in a 12-hour window Jul 10-11; Lloyd's List Intelligence reported no large vessels (>10,000 dwt) on the US-coordinated Southern Highway with AIS on since Jul 7. Iran re-declared the strait 'closed until further notice' after fresh US strikes; CENTCOM/US dispute the closure claim and report the Omani corridor remains operational. Iran and Oman agreed Jul 11 to continue technical navigation talks. Brent crude ~$78-79/bbl on Jul 13 (Al Jazeera/Fortune, Jul 13 2026) - elevated but well below the ~$107 May 2026 peak, inconsistent with a full closure.

items.label: New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)
items.rationale: Four or more distinct new major-economy measures (EU tyres AD, EU steel quota/parcel fee, Canada AD/CVD, USTR Section 301 forced-labor proposal) are confirmed within the trailing 3-month window, satisfying the '>=4 new measures' threshold for level 2 (ESCALATING). However, the US-China bilateral managed truce remains nominally intact (Busan agreement framework), and China has not announced counter-measures to date, indicating escalation is breadth-driven rather than a full truce breakdown.
items.fact: In the trailing 3 months, at least 4 distinct new major-economy trade measures targeting China are confirmed: (1) EU definitive anti-dumping duties on passenger-car/light-lorry tyres from China (4.3%-45.3%), published Jul 7 2026 (policy.trade.ec.europa.eu); (2) EU reduction of duty-free steel quota + new €3 parcel customs charge on Chinese imports, effective Jul 1 2026 (Al Jazeera/EC); (3) Canada CITT additional AD/CVD duties on specific Chinese goods, Jul 1 2026 (pcbglobaltrade.com); (4) USTR proposed Section 301 forced-labor tariffs of 12.5% on China, published Jun 2 2026 with Jul 7 hearing (ustr.gov), designed to replace the expiring Section 122 by ~Jul 24 2026. A parallel USTR Section 301 excess-capacity investigation targeting China was initiated Mar 12 2026 and remains active.

dissent.view: China-Briefing notes that China and the US 'agreed in principle' on terms restraining new tariffs above the Busan-negotiated rate, and China stopped short of counter-measures to the Jun 2 Section 301 proposal, suggesting the bilateral channel remains a managed truce rather than an outright escalation.

items.label: Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
items.rationale: The truce is not lapsed (ruling out 2), but the April 2025 controls were never part of the suspension, and new entity-list designations of MP Materials and USA Rare Earth constitute fresh partial breach actions; volume data corroborate real trade suppression, satisfying the rubric-1 criterion of 'partial breach (e.g. new entity-list additions)' independent of the <90-day clock.
items.fact: The Nov-2025 US-China minerals truce (MOFCOM Announcements 55-58, 61-62 suspended through Nov 10, 2026) remains formally in force per White House fact sheet (Nov 5, 2025) and confirmed by a senior US official as 'still in effect' (Reuters/E&E News, May 12-13, 2026). However, China's April 2025 Announcement 18 controls on 7 heavy REEs were never suspended; Chinese customs data as of May 2026 show yttrium, dysprosium, terbium exports ~50% below pre-control levels; and China placed US firms MP Materials and USA Rare Earth on its export control list circa late June 2026 (Mexico Business News, ~3 weeks prior to July 13, 2026).

dissent.view: US officials (May 2026) characterized the truce as 'still in effect' and framed the gap on April-2025 controls as an implementation dispute rather than a breach, which could support a score of 0 if entity-list additions are deemed outside truce scope.

items.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
items.rationale: The regime is case-by-case/whitelist (general licences cover only a handful of pre-cleared exporters, not most volume), processing times structurally exceed 30 days (60-120+ days in practice), and new categories were added to the control list within the trailing 3 months (Jan 2026 catalogue, June 2026 entity listings) — all three SLOWING triggers satisfied. FROZEN (2) is not warranted because licences continue to be issued to civilian commercial buyers and no sector-wide or country-wide suspension has been announced. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: MOFCOM Announcement No. 18 (April 2025) on 7 heavy/medium REEs and magnets is fully in force and was never suspended (CSIS, May 2026); statutory review is 45 working days but industry data (Duramag; logistics guide July 2026) show heavy-REE approvals routinely run 60-120+ days; general licences issued Dec 2025 cover only ~3 named Chinese magnet makers and are 'not broadly available'; Jan 2026 catalogue expansion added samarium/gadolinium/lutetium compounds as new controlled categories; June 22 2026 MOFCOM Announcement No. 23 placed MP Materials and USA Rare Earth on the entity control list with categorical supply denial; MOFCOM Announcement No. 26 (effective July 1 2026) formalised active enforcement reporting. Sources: CSIS rare-earth-export-restrictions-one-year-later (May 2026); Morgan Lewis LawFlash (July 2026); gettransport.com logistics guide (July 2026).

dissent.view: White House characterised Dec 2025 general licences as 'de facto removal' of controls, implying closer to FLOWING; Beijing has not confirmed this framing and civilian approval data do not support it.

Rare Earth Exchanges multi-institutional analysis reports licensing approval rates below 25% for European firms and sixfold price spikes, suggesting conditions approaching FROZEN for some buyers/sectors.

items.label: Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)
items.rationale: The rubric's EXTREME threshold requires <10% outside Taiwan AND no operational overseas leading-edge fab; all <=3nm TSMC capacity is currently 100% Taiwan-based with no overseas <=3nm fab yet in production, satisfying the EXTREME condition unambiguously. Arizona's sole overseas operational fab runs 4nm, which is above the <=3nm threshold, and no <=3nm overseas fab reaches volume production until H2 2027 at the earliest.
items.fact: As of July 2026, TSMC has zero operational <=3nm capacity outside Taiwan. Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1 produces 4nm (N4) at ~20,000-30,000 wpm but no <=3nm. Arizona Phase 2 (3nm) construction completed April 2026 with tool installation beginning Q3 2026 and volume production targeted H2 2027. Kumamoto Japan 3nm targeted 2028. All TSMC 2nm (N2, ~90,000-100,000 wpm) and 3nm (~180,000 wpm by end-2026) capacity is in Taiwan (Hsinchu, Tainan, Kaohsiung). Sources: TrendForce 2026-04-16, Focus Taiwan CNA 2026-04-16, Taipei Times 2026-04-29, tech-insider.org 2026-06-04, Wikipedia TSMC Arizona.

dissent.view: Some sources (Digitimes Jan 2026, tech-insider.org) report Arizona Phase 2 may be accelerated to produce 2nm rather than 3nm, pulling timeline to end-2027; this does not change the current (mid-2026) zero overseas <=3nm operational status but slightly alters the diversification trajectory.

items.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: Regular median-line crossings and double-digit PLAN ship presence constitute assertive grey-zone patrols exceeding 'routine,' mapping to Level 1; however, no named blockade rehearsal or large-scale encirclement exercise is underway in July 2026, and no kinetic clash or actual blockade exists, ruling out Level 2. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: Taiwan MND (via GlobalSecurity, 2026-07-07): '4 sorties of PLA aircraft, 9 PLAN ships and 3 official ships operating around Taiwan were detected... 3 out of 4 sorties crossed the median line.' AEI/ISW (2026-07-10) separately confirms PLA ADIZ incursions have reverted to ~200/month pre-Lai baseline since early 2026, with 12 days in June recording zero cross-median incursions; no named large-scale encirclement drill active in July 2026.

dissent.view: PRC-Russia 'Joint Sea-2026' exercise (July 6-13 near Qingdao) may indirectly enhance Taiwan invasion capabilities per AEI/ISW, but it is geographically distinct from Taiwan Strait and not a Taiwan-specific blockade drill.

items.label: Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)
items.rationale: No major cable is currently severed and unrepaired in the queried corridors (Red Sea, Hormuz, Baltic), ruling out level 2. However, Iran's documented, credible threats against Hormuz cables via state-linked IRGC media and heightened operational risk from military activity in the Gulf constitute a credible threat condition, matching level 1 (THREATENED).
items.fact: As of late April–July 2026, no major intercontinental submarine cable in the Red Sea, Hormuz, or Baltic is confirmed currently severed and unrepaired. Iran's IRGC-linked Tasnim News Agency issued veiled threats against Hormuz cables (Apr 23 2026); TeleGeography (Apr 2026) and Modern Diplomacy (Apr 28 2026) confirm 'subsea cables have not yet suffered damage' from the Iran conflict. The most recent actual confirmed cut is Taiwan's minor Matsu No. 3 cable (Beigan-Dongyin, ~1,500 residents affected, late Apr 2026). The last major Red Sea cuts (SEA-ME-WE-4, IMEWE, FALCON GCX, EIG) were Sep 6 2025 and are in repair/rerouting status. TeleGeography Submarine Cable Map last updated Jul 3 2026 shows no new emergency outage notices. Sources: Modern Diplomacy Apr 28 2026 (moderndiplomacy.eu); AGBI Apr 15 2026 (agbi.com); The Diplomat May 21 2026 (thediplomat.com); TeleGeography submarinecablemap.com Jul 3 2026.

dissent.view: Australian Defence Minister Marles (May 31 2026) stated undersea cables 'are being cut at an unprecedented rate' and cited 'attacks against subsea critical infrastructure at a scale and frequency that is historically unprecedented over the past 18 months,' implying ongoing active cutting beyond what is individually confirmed.

items.label: AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Multiple confirmed DOE-authorized curtailment orders specifically targeting data-center load under declared grid emergencies satisfy the rubric's top band unambiguously; this is not a forecast or warning but an executed federal emergency action. IEA Electricity 2026 independently corroborates systemic strain: over 2,500 GW stalled in global queues and 'rising congestion-related curtailment' across power systems.
items.fact: U.S. DOE issued Section 202(c) emergency orders on May 18 and June 30, 2026, authorizing PJM Interconnection to curtail data centers and large loads (>=50 MW) with backup generation; on July 2, PJM forecast a record peak of 166,147 MW across 13 states and D.C., with Maximum Generation, Load Management, and Hot Weather alerts all active. Source: Utility Dive (utilitydive.com/news/pjm-doe-emergency-order-curtail-data-centers/820571/, May 19 2026) and Electric Choice (electricchoice.com/blog/pjm-emergency-order-heat-wave-2026/, July 2 2026).

dissent.view: Some analysts (dev/sustainability, May 2026) argue actual consumption growth may be lower than feared due to efficiency gains and the high attrition rate of interconnection queue projects (~77% withdrawn), implying the physical grid crisis may be overstated relative to headlines.

items.label: China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
items.rationale: The mid-March 2026 addition of NK-blend and broader phosphate bans constitutes a NEW/BROADENED restriction within the trailing 3 months from July 2026, meeting rubric level 2 (TIGHTENED). The partial urea quota reopening under a strict price floor does not constitute a lifting; phosphate suspension through August 2026 remains confirmed by NDRC-directed industry action.
items.fact: In mid-March 2026, Beijing banned exports of nitrogen-potassium fertilizer blends and additional phosphate varieties (Reuters/Bloomberg, March 19-21 2026), extending beyond the NDRC-directed December 2025 phosphate suspension through August 2026. Urea was partially reopened on May 26 via a tightly controlled quota of ~1.5-1.6M tonnes with a $660/t FOB price floor (Profercy/Keyuan, May 26-29 2026), but phosphate and NK blend bans remain active. International urea prices +~40% vs pre-war levels confirmed by multiple sources.

dissent.view: Philippines reported China indicated fertilizer exports would not be restricted, though Chinese authorities never publicly confirmed this position (Reuters, March 2026)
Urea quota re-opening (May 26, ~1.5-1.6M t, $660/t floor) could be read as partial easing for urea specifically, though restrictions on phosphate and NK blends remain

items.label: Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: The rubric awards Band 2 for either a statewide moratorium enacted OR >10 jurisdictions with enacted pauses; the confirmed count of enacted local moratoria (100+, per DLA Piper July 2026) overwhelmingly exceeds the 10-jurisdiction threshold, anchoring a Band 2 rating even without a signed statewide law.
items.fact: DLA Piper (July 2026, dlapiper.com) reports 'more than 100 local moratoria have been adopted nationwide' and 'at least 12 states have considered moratorium legislation'; NY legislature passed statewide moratorium bill S10642 on June 4 2026 (Senate 44-16, Assembly 102-39) but it remains unsigned by Gov. Hochul as of early July 2026; Maine's statewide moratorium was vetoed April 24 2026; no statewide moratorium is currently in force, but enacted local pauses include: Minneapolis MN, Denver CO, Baltimore City MD, Dubuque County IA, Bloomington IL, Normal IL, Iron County UT, Manitowoc County WI, Reno NV, Smithfield RI, Oneonta NY, East Fishkill NY, Lysander NY, 20+ Michigan localities, multiple Georgia counties - well exceeding 10 enacted jurisdictions.

dissent.view: datacenterbans.com (as of June 1 2026) characterised the statewide path as 'closed' and emphasised no broad statewide moratorium had passed, highlighting that local pace was the primary front - a reading that slightly underweights the sheer number of enacted local pauses in assigning systemic severity.

items.label: Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)
items.rationale: The latest (April 2026) ICSG official forecast is a refined copper surplus of +96,000t for 2026, which exceeds the +100kt surplus threshold in the rubric. Under the rubric, a forecast surplus maps directly to score 0.
items.fact: ICSG 'Copper Market Forecast 2026-2027' (April 2026 Lisbon meeting): global refined copper surplus of ~96,000 metric tons forecast for 2026, reversing the prior October 2025 deficit forecast of 150,000t, driven by lower-than-expected demand growth (usage now +1.6% vs prior +2.1%) and higher secondary/scrap refined output. Source: icsg.org (April 2026 press release), corroborated by Recycling Today (Apr 28 2026), ADMIS (Apr 24 2026), Metalnomist (Jul 2026).

dissent.view: ICSG October 2025 forecast projected a 150,000t refined copper DEFICIT for 2026 — superseded by the April 2026 update
J.P. Morgan projected a 330,000t deficit for 2026, driven by AI/hyperscale data center copper demand
Goldman Sachs base case called for a 2026 surplus of ~160,000t, broadly aligning with updated ICSG direction

items.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Taiwan's WRA issued a confirmed water-rationing order directly affecting the Hsinchu chip-fab hub and postponed agricultural irrigation — both are confirmed production/supply curtailment acts satisfying the rubric's Level 2 threshold. The US Southwest simultaneously registers D3-D4 (extreme/exceptional) drought in the Four Corners farming region, independently also qualifying as Level 2.
items.fact: Taiwan WRA imposed water rationing affecting chipmaking hub Hsinchu County (March 2026, driest winter in 75 years); Tsengwen Reservoir at 9.6% capacity as of June 2, 2026 — 3 percentage points above the 2021 record low — with irrigation for the second rice crop officially postponed and TSMC CEO C.C. Wei warning of water constraints in June 2026 (Taipei Times, 2026-03-27; Taiwan News, 2026-06-02; Astute Group citing Reuters, June 2026). US Southwest concurrently shows D3-D4 drought in Four Corners states per US Drought Monitor map dated 2026-07-07 (droughtmonitor.unl.edu).

dissent.view: TSMC stated in 2026 it has contingency water recycling plans and no confirmed fab output curtailment has been publicly quantified, suggesting operational impact may be partial rather than acute

items.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
items.rationale: Over 5 years equates to >60 months median IR-to-COD wait, up from ~55 months (2024 cohort) per the prior edition; 60 months is the conservative floor of the stated range and is the single load-bearing figure mapped directly from the LBNL primary source.
items.fact: LBNL Queued Up 2026 Edition (data through end of 2025, published July 1 2026): 'the median duration from IR to COD was over 5 years for projects built in 2025.' Source: https://emp.lbl.gov/queues

dissent.view: 55 months median for projects reaching COD in 2024, per LBNL 2025 Edition (EOY-2024 data); this is the prior-year cohort figure, not the latest 2025-cohort reading
>40 months median for interconnection studies alone (not full IR-to-COD) as of 2024, per secondary analysis citing LBNL data

items.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
items.rationale: The rubric requires rating 2 only if the strait is 'CLOSED' AND a market signal corroborates it, and stipulates that a signed truce or convoys moving cap the rating at 1. However, the June 17 MOU has effectively collapsed under renewed July 7-12 hostilities; transit has dropped to near-zero (6 crossings in 12 hours); Brent is +4% and elevated to ~$79, providing a corroborating price signal; and Iran's formal 'closed until further notice' declaration is the operative sovereign statement, with the Omani lane practically inaccessible to large commercial vessels (no vessel >10,000 DWT has transited the Southern Highway with AIS on since July 7 per Lloyd's List). On balance the strait has re-approached a functional closure state with a corroborating oil move, but residual dark-ship transits and JMIC's claim the Omani lane is 'available' introduce sufficient ambiguity to hold medium confidence rather than high.
items.fact: IMF PortWatch (July 5): commercial transit at 39% of pre-crisis volume (34 vs ~88 vessels/day); Windward data July 10-11 shows only 6 vessel crossings in a 12-hour window vs 18-22/day earlier in July; Iran declared strait 'closed until further notice' after IRGC missile attacks on tankers July 7-12; JMIC (July 12) says Omani southern lane 'still available' but threat level 'severe' with mine danger; Brent crude +4% to $78.82/barrel on July 13 (Al Jazeera, July 13 2026), corroborating supply-disruption premium. Fertilizer/sulfur/ammonia flows have been near-halted since Feb 28 conflict onset per FAO/NDSU/World Bank (3-4M tonnes/month restrained; fertilizer price index at Oct-2022 highs).

items.label: Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Three top-20 mines (Grasberg #2, Kamoa-Kakula top-5, El Teniente top-10) have confirmed, physically active production cuts simultaneously in mid-2026, and the ICSG has officially flipped its 2026 market forecast to a deficit — both prongs of the CLUSTER threshold are satisfied.
items.fact: As of Q2 2026 (Ivanhoe July 8 2026 release), Kamoa-Kakula produced only 64,328t in Q2 with Phase 1&2 concentrators batch-operated and 2026 guidance at 290k-330kt vs original ~600kt target (~50% cut). Grasberg (Freeport 10-Q filed 2026) produced only 95M lb copper in Q1 2026 vs ~425M lb normal, with full restart delayed to early 2028 per mining.com May 8 2026. El Teniente (Codelco) carried ~25,000t production loss into 2026 from July 2025 fatal collapse per Fastmarkets Oct 2025. ICSG revised 2026 refined copper balance from +209kt surplus to -150kt deficit (Fastmarkets/ICSG, Oct 8 2025).

dissent.view: Kamoa-Kakula Q2 2026 output of 64,328t, while below prior targets, is partially an accounting change (concentrate->anode basis); some analysts note the mine is still producing meaningfully and H2 recovery is expected

items.label: Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The IWT is contested/suspended (abeyance since April 2025, reaffirmed July 2026) and official war-threat language has been exchanged by Pakistan's defence minister and India's water minister, squarely matching rubric level 1 — treaty contested + official threats, no confirmed kinetic action or deliberate physical cut-off carried out.
items.fact: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) remains formally 'in abeyance' as of July 2026: India reaffirmed on July 3 that the treaty stays suspended 'until Pakistan completely stops cross-border terrorism'; Pakistan's Defence Minister Khawaja Asif warned on ~June 20 that Pakistan 'would go to war' if water security is breached; India's water minister stated 'not a single drop of water will flow to Pakistan'; and a Court of Arbitration May 2026 award affirming limits on India's water-control ability was rejected by India as 'illegal'. No deliberate physical flow cut-off or armed clash confirmed. (Sources: CNBC / The Diplomat / Eastern Herald / Britannica, June–July 2026.)

dissent.view: Pakistan's abnormal Chenab flow variations (reported twice in 2025, once May 2026) could indicate a de-facto partial diversion already underway, which would push toward level 2; however no confirmed sustained deliberate cut-off is documented — Chatham House and CNBC treat these as 'abrupt variations', not a confirmed cut-off.

items.label: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
items.rationale: All rules remain at NPRM/comment stage within five days of the July 18, 2026 statutory deadline, with the Federal Reserve (a primary regulator) having issued no proposed rule at all — fitting the rubric's 'GAPS' definition of rules still in proposed/comment stage near the deadline with documented inter-agency divergence in participation. No court stay or compliance-failure event has been identified, so score 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: As of July 13, 2026 — five days before the statutory July 18, 2026 rulemaking deadline — zero final rules have been issued by any of the six required agencies. OCC published its NPRM on March 2, 2026 (comment period closed May 1); FDIC published its prudential NPRM on April 10, 2026 (comments closed June 9); FinCEN/OFAC published a joint AML NPRM on April 9, 2026 (comments closed June 9); NCUA published a supplemental NPRM (comments closing July 17, 2026 — the day before the deadline). The Federal Reserve Board, a named primary federal payment stablecoin regulator, had not published any proposed rule for applications or prudential frameworks as of the most recent reporting (KPMG reg alert, May 2026; CryptoTimes, May 18, 2026). The FDIC's comment period on one of its rules closes August 4, 2026 — two weeks after the statutory finalization deadline — making timely FDIC finalization procedurally impossible without bypassing its own comment process. Sources: OCC Bulletin 2026-3 (occ.gov); Federal Register 2026-04089 (Mar 2, 2026); FDIC.gov press release (Apr 7, 2026); Treasury/FinCEN press release sb0435 (Apr 9, 2026); VaaSBlock analysis citing Fed non-participation (Jun 9, 2026); Chapman & Cutler rulemaking tracker (updated Jun 1, 2026).

items.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
items.rationale: The latest Trepp property-type breakdown for June 2026 shows the CMBS office delinquency rate at 11.57%, up 4 bps month-over-month; this is the most recent hard data point and maps directly to the rubric's request for the latest percent figure.
items.fact: Trepp June 2026 CMBS report: 'office edged up four basis points to 11.57%' — MBA Newslink / Trepp, July 2026

items.label: GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Banks are actively offloading AI/data-center construction debt at a discount (Oracle project, named) and Oracle carries dual rating watches plus a 16-year CDS high — meeting rubric-1 criteria squarely. No issuer has yet experienced a confirmed default or distressed exchange, so level 2 is not triggered.
items.fact: FT reported (May 3-6 2026) that JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, SMBC and MUFG spent 6+ months distributing $38B of Oracle-leased data-center construction debt in Texas and Wisconsin, with 'some banks seeking to sell at a discount to non-bank lenders' (resultsense.com summarizing FT, May 6 2026). Separately, S&P and Moody's placed Oracle on negative credit watch with Oracle 5-yr CDS reaching a 16-year high of ~198bps in March 2026 (quinnemanuel.com, March 16 2026). CoreWeave carried a $4.2B 2026 refinancing wall but successfully closed DDTL 4.0 ($8.5B, A3/Moody's) on March 31 2026 and DDTL 5.0 ($3.1B, Ba2/Moody's) on May 18 2026, both oversubscribed. No confirmed default, distressed exchange, or failed refinancing found as of July 13 2026.

items.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
items.rationale: The documented state count of active non-renewal waves (~13 states per ClimateCosmos June 2026, corroborated by AgencyHeight March 2026 listing CA/FL/LA/TX/CO/NC) places this squarely in the 5-15 state band (rubric score 1). No top-10 insurer has fully exited a major state in 2026 (ruling out score 2), and AM Best Stable outlook plus FL reinsurance softening (-15-25% at June 2026 renewals) confirm some markets are stabilizing, consistent with rubric score 1 language.
items.fact: ClimateCosmos (June 2026) identifies 13 states as 'ground zero for the 2026 nonrenewal wave' (CA, FL, LA, CO, NE, OK, TX, NC, MN, GA, and others); NAIC issued a nationwide homeowners data call in March 2026 (June 15 submission deadline) to monitor non-renewals at ZIP-code level, citing systemic availability concerns; NAIC MCAS data (via Weiss Ratings/National Mortgage News 2026) shows FL non-renewed 3.35% and CA 3.18% of HO policies in 2024 at highest rates in nation. No top-10 insurer has fully exited a major state market in 2026; State Farm paused new CA business and non-renewed ~72K policies but remains active; FL Citizens depopulated from 1.42M (Oct 2023) to ~385K-395K policies (Jan 2026); AM Best revised US HO segment outlook from Negative to Stable (Dec 2025).

items.label: Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
items.rationale: France has a sitting PM governing without a parliamentary majority, repeatedly using Article 49.3 and surviving multiple no-confidence votes — the textbook definition of rubric band 1 (FRAGILE). No government collapse or snap election has occurred, ruling out band 2.
items.fact: PM Sébastien Lecornu leads a weak minority government. The most recent government-threatening event was on 2 February 2026, when two no-confidence motions (LFI: 260 votes; RN: 135 votes) failed to reach the 289-vote threshold needed to topple the government, allowing the 2026 budget to pass. The government had also repeatedly invoked Article 49.3 to force budget measures through parliament. No snap election has been called and no confidence vote has succeeded. (Sources: Al Jazeera 2026-02-02; Euronews 2026-01-23; Wikipedia 'Sébastien Lecornu', updated July 2026)

items.label: Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
items.rationale: Both primary regulators (TPR, ESMA) report improved buffers, reduced leverage, and normal operations through 2025-mid-2026 with no active stress episode; this aligns squarely with rubric band 0 (buffers adequate, no margin stress). ESMA's residual caveat about 'a subset of funds' facing potential challenges is a forward-looking stress-test flag, not an observed margin event, insufficient to elevate to band 1.
items.fact: ESMA TRV article dated 23 June 2026 (ESMA50-1949966494-4208) finds that LDI funds' ability to meet liquidity demands 'has improved compared to September 2022' after AIFMD Article 25 leverage measures, with NAV falling to EUR 113bn at end-2024 vs EUR 130bn at crisis; TPR Market Oversight report (data to 31 March 2025) confirms LDI market halved to ~£0.7trn, rate buffers raised to ~300bps, and 'LDI related operations have been normal' during 2025 rate volatility episodes. No forced gilt selling, margin-call episode, or central-bank intervention reported in 2025-2026.

items.label: Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)
items.rationale: Two of the three major agencies (Fitch on 12 Sep 2025, S&P on 17 Oct 2025) executed actual rating cuts from AA- to A+ within the trailing 12 months, satisfying rubric band 2 (DOWNGRADE). Moody's negative outlook would independently satisfy band 1, but band 2 takes precedence.
items.fact: S&P lowered France to 'A+' from 'AA-' on 17 Oct 2025 (spglobal.com, sourceId/101651950); Fitch downgraded France to 'A+' from 'AA-' on 12 Sep 2025 (edhec.edu/internationalbanker.com); Moody's kept Aa3 but shifted outlook to negative on 24 Oct 2025 (ratings.moodys.com/453186). All actions within trailing 12 months of 2026-07-13.

items.label: Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)
items.rationale: The load-bearing measured multiple is 4.4× (Benchmark, 2025 actuals) trending higher through 2026; the Argus/Reuters June 2026 corroboration of ×4–×5 places the mid-2026 reading at approximately 4.5×, consistent with the upward trajectory between the 4.4× 2025 baseline and the 8.3× 2027 forecast.
items.fact: Benchmark Minerals (24 Mar 2026): 'dysprosium oxide prices on a CIF North America basis were 4.4 times that of EXW China prices' in 2025, with forecast of 8.3× by 2027; corroborated by Argus data cited by Reuters/Oregon Group (10 Jun 2026) that dysprosium and terbium oxide prices outside China 'climbed x4 to x5' since April 2025 export controls.

items.label: US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
items.rationale: Two named US domestic rare-earth producers (MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc.) are on China's MOFCOM export-control list as of 22 June 2026, satisfying rubric level 1. The restriction is firm-specific (10 named entities across multiple sectors), not a sector-wide or categorical ban on US rare-earth producers as a class, so level 2 does not apply.
items.fact: China MOFCOM Announcement No. 23 of 2026, issued 22 June 2026, added 10 named US entities to its export control list under the Export Control Law and Dual-Use Items Regulations, effective immediately. The two US rare-earth producers explicitly named are MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc. The action is entity-specific: it bars Chinese exporters from supplying dual-use items to those firms and prohibits any party worldwide from transferring China-origin dual-use goods to them, but does not impose a categorical ban on the US rare-earth sector as a class. Market signal: share-price reaction on announcement day was muted (MP Materials roughly flat, USA Rare Earth modestly higher), consistent with an entity-level rather than sector-wide restriction. Source: MOFCOM announcement No. 23/2026 (https://www.mofcom.gov.cn/zwgk/zcfb/art/2026/art_dfa9cc5c1e004d7fbb86f83d249e7986.html), corroborated by Al Jazeera, Benchmark Minerals, Arnold & Porter advisory, SMM, and Reuters market data, all dated 22 June 2026.

items.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: The index is NOT falling on a YoY basis (+2.2%), ruling out rubric band 0. The MoM decline is only -0.3%, well below the 3% threshold for the upper boundary of band 1, and the index remains elevated above year-ago levels; there is no MoM increase >=5% or 3-month rise >=10%, ruling out band 2. Band 1 (ELEVATED) applies.
items.fact: FAO Food Price Index (FFPI) averaged 130.3 points in June 2026, down 0.3% (0.4 points) MoM from May's 130.8, but +2.2% YoY above its year-earlier level. Source: FAO official release, fao.org/worldfoodsituation/foodpricesindex, published 3 July 2026.

items.label: Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
items.rationale: Storage at 29% of capacity places Lake Mead below the rubric's 30% CRITICAL threshold; the elevation of ~1,043 ft has already breached (not merely approached) the 1,050 ft Hoover Dam minimum power pool, constituting a confirmed physical impact on hydropower generation. Both criteria independently satisfy the top band (score 2).
items.fact: USBR data (via lakeinsights.com/USBR gauge feed, Jul 10 2026): Lake Mead elevation = 1,042.84 ft; USBR Weekly Hydrologic Update (Jun 29 2026) confirms storage at 29% of capacity. Newsweek citing USBR: 'Lake Mead was just 29 percent full, with 7,487,010 acre-feet in storage.' Hoover Dam minimum power pool = 1,050 ft; current elevation is ~7 ft BELOW that threshold.

flags.name: Two Hormuz reads disagree on the same strait
flags.message: Gulf fertilizer inputs are rated CUT (2, i.e. closure) but the sea-lane indicator rates the very same Strait of Hormuz only CONTESTED (<=1). They track one physical chokepoint and must agree - align the fertilizer read down to DISRUPTED (1) unless the strait is genuinely closed.

indicators.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)

flags.name: Redemption gates widespread, but HY spreads benign
flags.message: Large-fund redemption gates are rated WIDESPREAD (2) but high-yield spreads are benign. Broad gating usually coincides with visible credit stress; confirm the gates are systemic, not a couple of isolated funds.

indicators.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)

flags.name: Gulf fertilizer inputs rated cut, but crude is benign
flags.message: Gulf input flow is rated CUT (2) but crude is benign. Same chokepoint, so a true cut should co-move with oil. Reconcile this with the Hormuz status read.

indicators.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)

s1_basis_trade.label: Hidden leverage in US government bonds
s1_basis_trade.gist: Hedge funds borrow heavily to trade US government bonds; if that trade unwinds fast it can freeze the market everyone treats as safe.

s2_japan.label: Japan's rates and the global cash carry trade
s2_japan.gist: Cheap Japanese money has funded bets worldwide for years; as Japan's interest rates rise, pulling that money home can ripple through global markets.

s3_private_credit.label: Private lending you can't see into
s3_private_credit.gist: A fast-growing pile of loans sits outside the banks with little public disclosure, so stress there is hard to spot until it is already large.

s4_dev_debt.label: Debt stress in developing countries
s4_dev_debt.gist: Many poorer nations owe more than they can comfortably repay; a wave of defaults can spread and pull in their lenders.

s5_china_deflation.label: China's prices spilling into the world
s5_china_deflation.gist: Big swings in China's factory prices - falling or rising - get exported to everyone else as cheaper goods or higher input costs.

s6_rare_earths.label: Rare-earth minerals used as a weapon
s6_rare_earths.gist: China controls most of the world's rare-earth processing and is now restricting it, squeezing US defense and high-tech manufacturing.

s7_stablecoins.label: Crypto dollars and US government debt
s7_stablecoins.gist: Stablecoins must hold US Treasuries as backing, so their rapid growth - or a sudden wobble - now feeds straight back into the government-debt market.

s8_ai_capex.label: The AI spending boom
s8_ai_capex.gist: Enormous borrowing to build AI data centers is racing ahead of the revenue meant to justify it, concentrating the risk in a few giant companies.

s9_cre.label: Commercial real estate and small banks
s9_cre.gist: Half-empty offices and loans coming due strain the regional banks that hold them - a slow-burning credit risk.

s10_france.label: France and eurozone stability
s10_france.gist: A politically gridlocked, heavily indebted France is the soft spot that could reopen broader euro-area stress.

s11_consumer_debt.label: Household debt strain
s11_consumer_debt.gist: Rising late payments on car loans and buy-now-pay-later show households stretching - an early warning on consumer health.

s12_gold_dedollar.label: Gold and moving away from the dollar
s12_gold_dedollar.gist: Central banks stockpiling gold and building non-dollar payment systems slowly chip away at the dollar's dominance.

s13_insurance.label: Insurers pulling back
s13_insurance.gist: As disaster losses climb, insurers raise prices or quit whole regions, leaving homes and assets exposed.

s14_pensions.label: Pensions and an aging population
s14_pensions.gist: Aging populations and underfunded pensions strain public budgets; leveraged pension funds can also break in a sudden rate shock, as the UK saw in 2022.

s15_ai_power.label: AI's strain on the power grid
s15_ai_power.gist: AI data centers need more electricity than the grid can quickly supply, triggering curtailments, building moratoria, and multi-year connection queues.

s16_copper.label: Copper for electrification
s16_copper.gist: Electrifying everything needs far more copper than mines can deliver, and major mine outages are tightening supply further.

s17_water.label: Water as a hidden constraint
s17_water.gist: Droughts and shrinking reservoirs threaten chip factories, farms, and power generation - an underpriced input with no substitute.

s18_food_fertilizer.label: Food and fertilizer supply
s18_food_fertilizer.gist: Export curbs and shipping disruptions to fertilizer raise the risk of higher food prices down the line.

s19_lithography.label: The chipmaking chokepoint
s19_lithography.gist: The most advanced chips depend on one Dutch tool-maker and Taiwan's factories; any disruption there hits the entire tech economy.

s20_sea_lanes.label: Shipping lanes and undersea cables
s20_sea_lanes.gist: Key straits like Hormuz and the undersea cables carrying the internet are vulnerable chokepoints for global trade and data.

s21_clearinghouses.label: The financial system's plumbing
s21_clearinghouses.gist: Clearinghouses sit at the center of every market; if one seized up under stress, it would jam trading everywhere at once.

playbook.disclaimer: Educational cause-and-effect only - how stress like this has historically moved through the system. Nothing here is investment advice or a prediction.

s1_basis_trade.mechanism: A rates shock forces highly leveraged government-bond arbitrage positions to unwind at once: margin calls trigger forced Treasury selling, liquidity in the world's benchmark market evaporates, and everything priced off it reprices.
benefits: cash and short-term bills through the panic
long-term government bonds AFTER central-bank intervention
s1_basis_trade.watch: The funding-spread and bond-volatility rows above; the historical pivot was the central bank announcing a backstop facility.
s1_basis_trade.trap: In past episodes the 'safe' asset itself fell first - forced sellers sell what they can - so the flight-to-safety instinct lost money until the intervention.

s2_japan.mechanism: Surging Japanese yields pull decades of cheap yen funding home: the yen spikes on repatriation, and leveraged positions worldwide that were financed in yen get liquidated together - hitting high-momentum equities and emerging markets hardest.
benefits: Japanese banks and insurers as rates normalize
s2_japan.watch: The 30-year JGB row above (live official feed) and the yen; past unwinds announced themselves through the currency.
s2_japan.trap: Historical carry unwinds were violent but brief - the 2024 episode round-tripped within days; the durable shift was in Japanese financials, not the panic.

s3_private_credit.mechanism: One honest markdown forces everyone's marks: investor withdrawals hit gates, gated funds fire-sell into an illiquid market, and lending to mid-sized companies contracts just as they need to refinance.
bleeds: private-credit vehicles trading below stated asset value
benefits: holders of dry powder buying discounted credit afterwards
public high-quality credit as the flight destination
s3_private_credit.watch: The default-rate and redemption-gate rows above; public high-yield spreads are the honest proxy for hidden marks.
s3_private_credit.trap: Opacity means the repricing arrives as a sudden gap, not a trend - by the time it is public, the move has largely happened.

s4_dev_debt.mechanism: A cluster of sovereign defaults - not one - overwhelms the restructuring machinery: redemptions from emerging-market debt funds force indiscriminate selling of strong and weak credits alike, and stress spreads through common creditors.
benefits: and historically the buyers of defaulted debt after restructuring - among the best-documented recoveries in fixed income
s4_dev_debt.watch: The new-IMF-programs row above and the dollar's strength, which is usually the upstream cause.
s4_dev_debt.trap: Index diversification means one or two defaults barely register; the systemic wave this story warns of is exactly what official bailouts exist to prevent.

s5_china_deflation.mechanism: Deepening factory-gate deflation is exported as cheaper goods, compressing margins for competing producers everywhere - unless Beijing's stimulus response whipsaws the pressure into a sudden commodity and China-equity rebound.
benefits: long-term bonds in the deflation leg
Chinese equities and industrial metals in the stimulus leg
s5_china_deflation.watch: The producer-price row above and any stimulus announcement - the two legs point opposite directions.
s5_china_deflation.trap: Policy whipsaw: historically every stimulus rumor violently reversed the deflation trade within days.

s6_rare_earths.mechanism: Export restrictions tighten into denial: magnet and heavy-rare-earth supply to defense, vehicle, and robotics manufacturers halts, panic stockpiling begins, and processing capacity outside China re-rates from commodity business to strategic asset.
bleeds: vehicle and defense production schedules in the short run
s6_rare_earths.watch: The license-flow, blacklist, and ex-China premium rows above - the premium is the market's own severity gauge.
s6_rare_earths.trap: Historically these moves reversed as fast as they started on any truce headline, and diversified funds in the space often hold the Chinese producers too.

s7_stablecoins.mechanism: A run on a major stablecoin forces its reserves - tens of billions in short-term government debt - to liquidate within days, briefly dislocating the bill market while the crypto ecosystem's dollar rails seize and leveraged positions cascade.
benefits: historically the surviving issuer gained lasting market share after each depeg scare
s7_stablecoins.watch: The live peg-deviation and supply rows above (updated daily from the aggregator) - past runs announced themselves in the peg first.
s7_stablecoins.trap: Crypto markets have a long history of face-ripping rebounds inside downtrends; past episodes punished conviction in both directions.

s8_ai_capex.mechanism: The debt-financed buildout meets a revenue shortfall: one credit event at a leveraged data-center operator reprices all AI-adjacent borrowing, capital-spending guidance gets cut, chip orders fall, and power-demand forecasts deflate - the whole complex moves together because it was funded together.
bleeds: power producers priced on AI demand
benefits: and - the dot-com lesson - the post-bust survivors compounded for a decade
s8_ai_capex.watch: The capex-gap, revenue-gap, and refinancing rows above; credit stress historically led equity repricing.
s8_ai_capex.trap: Bubbles run longer than solvency: betting against this one early has been the era's classic ruin trade, which is why timing signals matter more than valuation.

s9_cre.mechanism: Office refinancing failures cluster into bank failures: deposit flight spreads to lookalike regional lenders, credit to small business contracts, and commercial-property marks finally reflect reality.
benefits: the largest banks via deposit inflows
and historically the whole sector rebounded within days of a backstop
s9_cre.watch: The bank-failure and office-delinquency rows above and the official problem-bank list.
s9_cre.trap: The 2023 backstop bottomed the sector in 48 hours - the policy response, not the failure, was the historical turning point.

s10_france.mechanism: Fiscal paralysis pushes French borrowing costs through crisis thresholds: banks holding the government's own bonds absorb the losses, the sovereign-bank doom loop reawakens, and stress spreads across euro-area borrowing costs.
bleeds: other high-debt euro members through contagion
benefits: and non-euro safe havens in every euro crisis
s10_france.watch: The OAT-Bund spread row above (live feed) - it is the market's single-number verdict on this story.
s10_france.trap: The central bank: euro-breakup positioning has been ruined repeatedly by ECB backstops built precisely for this scenario.

s11_consumer_debt.mechanism: Subprime stress spreads upward: rising late payments become charge-offs, lenders tighten, discretionary spending cracks, and the weakness travels from consumer lenders to retailers to the broad economy.
benefits: trade-down retailers in every past consumer squeeze
s11_consumer_debt.watch: The subprime-delinquency and BNPL rows above; the staples-versus-discretionary ratio is the market's own recession gauge.
s11_consumer_debt.trap: Lenders provision ahead of losses - their shares historically bottomed while delinquencies were still rising.
